description:
You are an on-call incident responder who has just received an alert about sporadic system slowness.  
    Your job is to capture a quick, point-in-time diagnostic snapshot so that another team can later
    analyse what was happening at (roughly) the moment of the alert.

    1. Create a directory called  
       /home/user/incident_logs  
       if it does not already exist.

    2. Inside that directory create a single **plain-text** file named  
       /home/user/incident_logs/incident_report.log

    3. The file must contain **five** clearly delimited sections, **in the exact order** shown below.
       Each section begins with a header line that consists of the word “===”, a single space, an
       UPPER-CASE identifier, another single space, and then “===”.  
       The header line must appear *exactly* as written (case-sensitive, no extra spaces,
       no surrounding quotes).  
       Immediately after each header, write a newline and then the required command output
       (captured at runtime) with no blank lines inserted between the header and its output.

       a. === DATE ===          → output of the command: date --iso=seconds  
       b. === UPTIME ===        → output of the command: uptime  
       c. === TOP ===           → the first 15 lines of the command: top -b -n 1  
       d. === MEMORY ===        → full output of the command: free -m  
       e. === DISK ===          → full output of the command: df -h

       Example outline (the actual data will differ):

       === DATE ===
       2025-01-03T12:34:56+00:00
       === UPTIME ===
       12:34:56 up  3:21,  1 user,  load average: 0.42, 0.37, 0.33
       === TOP ===
       top - 12:34:56 up 3:21,  1 user,  load average: 0.42, 0.37, 0.33
       Tasks: 113 total,   1 running, 112 sleeping,   0 stopped,   0 zombie
       %Cpu(s):  3.0 us,  1.0 sy,  0.0 ni, 95.0 id,  1.0 wa,  0.0 hi,  0.0 si,  0.0 st
       ...
       (exactly 15 lines from top)
       === MEMORY ===
                     total        used        free      shared  buff/cache   available
       Mem:           1995         355        1024          12         615        1388
       Swap:             0           0           0
       === DISK ===
       Filesystem      Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
       /dev/root        20G  6.5G   13G  34% /

    4. Make sure the resulting file has *at least* these five section headers in the correct order.
       The automated grader will:
       • check that /home/user/incident_logs/incident_report.log exists,  
       • confirm it is not empty, and  
       • verify that the five headers appear **exactly** as specified and in the required order.

    Notes & constraints:
    • You do NOT need sudo permissions; all requested commands work unprivileged.  
    • Keep the task concise—3 to 4 commands are sufficient (mkdir, date, uptime, top, free, df,
      along with shell redirection).  
    • Do not surround the header lines with quotes.  
    • Do not insert extra blank lines between headers and their command outputs.  
    • Ensure you run the commands at the moment you build the report so the snapshot is current.

    When you are done, you should have exactly one file,
    /home/user/incident_logs/incident_report.log, whose contents follow the format above.
truth:
The autograder will verify:

    1. Directory: /home/user/incident_logs exists and is a directory.
    2. File: /home/user/incident_logs/incident_report.log exists, is a regular file, and has
       non-zero size.
    3. Contents: the file contains, in this precise order, the five header lines:
          === DATE ===
          === UPTIME ===
          === TOP ===
          === MEMORY ===
          === DISK ===
       A simple regex check will ensure each header appears exactly once and that their sequence
       is correct. Any additional data (the command outputs) between the headers is allowed and is
       not validated beyond being non-empty.
name: task_000000_5251275e
